metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

records.notes: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Pale cap, pale pore surface maturing pinkish/brownish, bitter taste, and Tylopilus features are the field clues. Often treated in pine or pine-oak contexts.

records.mushroom_card_note: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.
records.field_identification: Pale cap, pale pore surface maturing pinkish/brownish, bitter taste, and Tylopilus features are the field clues. Often treated in pine or pine-oak contexts.
records.edibility_detail: Bitter bolete; do not spend table time on it.

images_structured.title: Pale Bitter Bolete supporting field view
Pale Bitter Bolete additional field view 1

records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food entry; use mainly as a recognizable pale bolete comparison.
records.notes: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits.
staining: Variable weak blue or pinkish staining
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall oak-associated bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Pale cap, smooth white stem, yellowish-to-olive pore surface, oak/hardwood association, and inconsistent bluish/pinkish staining are the checks.

records.mushroom_card_note: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits.
records.field_identification: Pale cap, smooth white stem, yellowish-to-olive pore surface, oak/hardwood association, and inconsistent bluish/pinkish staining are the checks.
records.edibility_detail: Occasional/niche; do not rely on bruising alone for ID.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a prime edible in this guide. Keep for comparison and cautious identification.
records.notes: Porphyrellus nebulosus: dark Tylopilus-like bolete with gray-brown tones; included mainly as a cautious comparison species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Porphyrellus nebulosus: dark Tylopilus-like bolete with gray-brown tones; included mainly as a cautious comparison species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated conservatively with eastern Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-type summer-and-fall boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Use the dark cap, darkening pore surface, spore color, host context, and bruising/staining behavior to separate this from other dusky boletes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Porphyrellus nebulosus: dark Tylopilus-like bolete with gray-brown tones; included mainly as a cautious comparison species.
records.field_identification: Use the dark cap, darkening pore surface, spore color, host context, and bruising/staining behavior to separate this from other dusky boletes.
records.edibility_detail: Use with caution; this is primarily an ID/comparison entry until field/photo support improves.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended here as a food target; useful mainly as a caution/comparison record for dark staining boletes.
records.notes: Porphyrellus sordidus: dark bolete that bruises blue-green to dark; keep out of the “easy edible bolete” bucket.

mushroom_profile.summary: Porphyrellus sordidus: dark bolete that bruises blue-green to dark; keep out of the “easy edible bolete” bucket.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated conservatively with eastern Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-type summer-and-fall boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look for the sordid brown cap, dark pore surface/spore-print group, and strong blue-green to dark staining when injured.

records.mushroom_card_note: Porphyrellus sordidus: dark bolete that bruises blue-green to dark; keep out of the “easy edible bolete” bucket.
records.field_identification: Look for the sordid brown cap, dark pore surface/spore-print group, and strong blue-green to dark staining when injured.
records.edibility_detail: Inedible/not recommended in this guide until better local confirmation says otherwise.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target in this guide; use as an identification/comparison record until the species is better documented locally.

mushroom_profile.summary: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included mainly for comparison.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; treated conservatively with other eastern Gyroporus/boletes until species-level Upper Michigan timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included as an ID/comparison species rather than a prime target.
records.field_identification: Check for Gyroporus traits: brittle/chalky flesh, stem that may become hollow, dry cap, pores rather than gills, and red to purplish tones. Compare against other Gyroporus and small red/brown boletes.
records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively. Gyroporus species boundaries and local relevance need confirmation before this belongs in any edible target list.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended because the taste is very bitter.
records.notes: Reddish-brown Bitter Bolete: handsome eastern Tylopilus, but very bitter; useful for ID practice, not the skillet.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish-brown Bitter Bolete: handsome eastern Tylopilus, but very bitter; useful for ID practice, not the skillet.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Purple-brown to reddish-brown cap, pinkish pores, bitter taste, and stem developing olive shades help separate it from mild Tylopilus species.
cap_surface: Purplish brown to reddish brown cap
stem_feature: Brownish stem with possible olive stains

records.mushroom_card_note: Reddish-brown Bitter Bolete: handsome eastern Tylopilus, but very bitter; useful for ID practice, not the skillet.
records.field_identification: Purple-brown to reddish-brown cap, pinkish pores, bitter taste, and stem developing olive shades help separate it from mild Tylopilus species.
records.edibility_detail: Bitter and not worth collecting for food.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible, but keep as a niche species unless you know it well.
records.notes: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated as a summer-and-fall eastern bolete until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look for the shaggy or jagged stem surface, yellow-brown to reddish-brown cap that may crack into patches, and oak/hemlock/pine associations.

records.mushroom_card_note: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.
records.field_identification: Look for the shaggy or jagged stem surface, yellow-brown to reddish-brown cap that may crack into patches, and oak/hemlock/pine associations.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible, but this guide treats it as a modest/niche bolete rather than a top food.
records.notes: Spotted/Affine Bolete: hardwood bolete with whitish pores maturing yellow-brown and no dramatic bruising.

mushroom_profile.summary: Spotted/Affine Bolete: hardwood bolete with whitish pores maturing yellow-brown and no dramatic bruising.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Brown cap sometimes with pale spots, whitish pores becoming yellow-brown, yellow-brown spore print, and no dramatic staining separate it from many olive-spored boletes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Spotted/Affine Bolete: hardwood bolete with whitish pores maturing yellow-brown and no dramatic bruising.
records.field_identification: Brown cap sometimes with pale spots, whitish pores becoming yellow-brown, yellow-brown spore print, and no dramatic staining separate it from many olive-spored boletes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible reputation exists, but use only after confident separation from similar boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Not a priority food mushroom in this guide; included for comparison within the Tylopilus group.
records.notes: Tylopilus appalachiensis: hardwood Tylopilus with brown-bruising pore surface; keep separate from mild edible-looking boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Tylopilus appalachiensis: hardwood Tylopilus with brown-bruising pore surface; keep separate from mild edible-looking boletes.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated conservatively with eastern Tylopilus summer-and-fall boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Use hardwood association, pore-surface bruising brown, pinkish Tylopilus pore maturity, and lack of dramatic reticulation to keep it separate.

records.mushroom_card_note: Tylopilus appalachiensis: hardwood Tylopilus with brown-bruising pore surface; keep separate from mild edible-looking boletes.
records.field_identification: Use hardwood association, pore-surface bruising brown, pinkish Tylopilus pore maturity, and lack of dramatic reticulation to keep it separate.
records.edibility_detail: Treat cautiously unless identified confidently by an experienced bolete person.

records.culinary_uses: Sometimes considered edible when confidently identified, but this guide treats it as a cautious bolete because Tylopilus look-alikes can be bitter or confusing.

mushroom_profile.summary: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; useful as a comparison species around bitter boletes.
staining: Pores bruise brown; flesh may slowly stain pinkish
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.edibility_detail: Potentially edible with confident ID; avoid if bitter, old, waterlogged, or uncertain.
records.field_identification: Look for a maroon to reddish-brown velvety cap, whitish pores becoming pinkish-tan, brown-bruising pores, and oak/hardwood association. Taste should be mild, not strongly bitter.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Oak association, mild taste, pore color, and brown bruising.

records.edibility_notes: Mild-tasting Tylopilus; edible reports exist, but confirm it is not a bitter Tylopilus before using.

edible_use.notes: Mild-tasting Tylopilus; edible reports exist, but confirm it is not a bitter Tylopilus before using.

records.mushroom_card_note: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; not the bitter-bolete disaster, but still an ID-first bolete.

records.culinary_uses: Potentially edible if confidently identified as a mild Tylopilus, but this guide treats it as a cautious/niche bolete.
records.notes: Tylopilus ferrugineus: rusty-brown mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores; advanced ID, not a casual basket filler.

mushroom_profile.summary: Tylopilus ferrugineus: rusty-brown mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores; advanced ID, not a casual basket filler.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated conservatively with eastern Tylopilus summer-and-fall boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look for rusty to reddish-brown cap/stem colors, Tylopilus pinkish pore maturity, mild taste, and hardwood association.
cap_surface: Rusty brown to reddish brown cap

records.mushroom_card_note: Tylopilus ferrugineus: rusty-brown mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores; advanced ID, not a casual basket filler.
records.field_identification: Look for rusty to reddish-brown cap/stem colors, Tylopilus pinkish pore maturity, mild taste, and hardwood association.
records.edibility_detail: Advanced identification recommended; do not confuse with bitter Tylopilus species.
